name: Entrepreneurs
description: Building, betting, and beginning — hard lessons from founders who bet on themselves.
author: Quips Editorial

quotes.content: Day 2 is stasis. Followed by irrelevance. Followed by excruciating, painful decline. Followed by death. And that is why it is always Day 1.
quotes.source: Amazon 2016 Letter to Shareholders (published April 2017)
quotes.notes: Bezos's warning against complacency at scale; verified verbatim on Amazon's official aboutamazon.com posting of the letter.

quotes.content: We believe that a fundamental measure of our success will be the shareholder value we create over the long term.
quotes.source: Amazon 1997 Letter to Shareholders, 'It's all about the long term'
quotes.notes: Opening line of the famous long-term section; verified verbatim on Amazon's official reprint of the original 1997 letter.

quotes.content: We will make bold rather than timid investment decisions where we see a sufficient probability of gaining market leadership advantages.
quotes.source: Amazon 1997 Letter to Shareholders
quotes.notes: From the 1997 letter's management-and-decision-making principles; confirmed against the letter's primary text.

quotes.content: When forced to choose between optimizing the appearance of our GAAP accounting and maximizing the present value of future cash flows, we'll take the cash flows.
quotes.source: Amazon 1997 Letter to Shareholders
quotes.notes: A core Bezos long-term-bet principle; confirmed against the primary 1997 letter text.

quotes.content: A brand for a company is like a reputation for a person. You earn reputation by trying to do hard things well.
quotes.source: Bloomberg BusinessWeek interview, 'Jeff Bezos on Word-of-Mouth Power' (2004)
quotes.notes: Often misattributed to a shareholder letter; it is actually from a 2004 Bloomberg BusinessWeek interview, confirmed on the bloomberg.com article page.

quotes.content: Your margin is my opportunity.
quotes.source: Attributed; reported in Brad Stone's 'The Everything Store' (2013)
quotes.notes: Source axis fails: reported in a reputable biography but with no primary letter, transcript, or on-record utterance; treat as reported rather than documented.

quotes.content: Every moment in business happens only once. The next Bill Gates will not build an operating system.
quotes.source: Zero to One: Notes on Startups, or How to Build the Future (2014), Preface
quotes.notes: Opening lines of the book's preface, arguing that copying past winners ('1 to n') is not creating the new ('0 to 1'); confirmed against the book text.

quotes.content: Monopoly is the condition of every successful business.
quotes.source: Zero to One (2014), Ch. 3
quotes.notes: Thiel's core claim that durable success means escaping competition; extracted directly from the book's full text.

quotes.content: Competition is for losers.
quotes.source: 'Competition Is for Losers,' The Wall Street Journal (Sept. 12, 2014), adapted from Zero to One
quotes.notes: Functions as the title and thesis of Thiel's own bylined WSJ essay; attribution to Thiel is solid.

quotes.content: Brilliant thinking is rare, but courage is in even shorter supply than genius.
quotes.source: Zero to One (2014), Preface
quotes.notes: Thiel argues the binding constraint on innovation is courage, not intelligence; wording confirmed against the book.

quotes.content: There are no silver bullets for this, only lead bullets.
quotes.source: 'Lead Bullets,' a16z blog (2011); also in 'The Hard Thing About Hard Things' (2014)
quotes.notes: The lesson that existential competitive threats are beaten by a better product, not a shortcut; verified on Horowitz's own a16z post.

quotes.content: The hard thing isn't setting a big, hairy, audacious goal. The hard thing is laying people off when you miss the big goal.
quotes.source: The Hard Thing About Hard Things (2014), Introduction
quotes.notes: From the book's opening litany on what actually makes running a company hard; wording is identical across all reproductions of the intro.

quotes.content: Startups take off because the founders make them take off.
quotes.source: Essay 'Do Things that Don't Scale' (2013), paulgraham.com
quotes.notes: Verified against Graham's own site; the full sentence begins 'Actually startups take off...'. Startups rarely grow by themselves — founders must push them into motion.

quotes.content: Make something people want.
quotes.source: Y Combinator motto; discussed in Graham's essay 'Be Good' (2008), paulgraham.com
quotes.notes: Y Combinator's founding motto, associated with Graham and appearing in his essays and YC materials.

quotes.content: If you're not embarrassed by the first version of your product, you've launched too late.
quotes.source: Reid Hoffman on X/Twitter (2017); rooted in LinkedIn's launch philosophy
quotes.notes: Hoffman restated his own maxim verbatim on his own X account; the concept traces to LinkedIn's early ship-fast approach.

quotes.content: In business, to be a success, you only have to be right once.
quotes.source: 'Success and Motivation – You only have to be right once!' blogmaverick.com (2005)
quotes.notes: The true verbatim line from Cuban's own blog; the popular one-line paraphrase conflates two non-adjacent sentences, so this restores the original wording.

quotes.content: Sweat equity is the best startup capital.
quotes.source: 'The Best Equity is Sweat Equity,' blogmaverick.com (2008)
quotes.notes: Verbatim from Cuban's blog (original: 'Sweat Equity is the best start up capital'); the widely-quoted 'most valuable equity there is' version is a paraphrase and was not used.

quotes.content: Every day some stranger from anywhere in the world that you have never met is trying to come up with a way to put you out of business.
quotes.source: 'The Sport of Business,' blogmaverick.com (2005)
quotes.notes: Cuban's own blog wording (original spells 'any where'); this is the documented source behind the polished 'work like someone is trying to take it away' aphorism.

quotes.content: If somebody offers you an amazing opportunity but you are not sure you can do it, say yes – then learn how to do it later.
quotes.source: Screw It, Let's Do It (2006); posted verbatim on Branson's own X account (2018)
quotes.notes: Branson's 'opportunity favours the bold' maxim; confirmed verbatim on his own X account and Virgin.com.

quotes.content: Screw it, let's do it.
quotes.source: Title of 'Screw It, Let's Do It: Lessons in Life' (2006); Branson's documented personal motto
quotes.notes: His action-over-hesitation mantra and the title of his own book.

quotes.content: Business opportunities are like buses, there's always another one coming.
quotes.source: Attributed to Branson (often cited to his memoirs)
quotes.notes: Source axis fails: found only in third-party books quoting him, never confirmed inside a Branson-authored primary source; likely genuine but not citable to a primary.

quotes.content: What did you fail at this week?
quotes.source: Recounted by Blakely, CNBC interview (2013)
quotes.notes: The dinner-table question Blakely's father asked her as a child, which she credits with reframing failure as effort rather than outcome; consistently recounted in her on-record interviews.

quotes.content: We are not in the coffee business serving people. We are in the people business serving coffee.
quotes.source: Widely attributed to Schultz (often to 'Pour Your Heart Into It', 1997)
quotes.notes: Source axis fails: full-text search did not locate it in his own books; the exact phrase surfaces only in third-party books attributing it to him.

quotes.content: Let everyone else call your idea crazy... just keep going. Don't stop. Don't even think about stopping until you get there, and don't give much thought to where 'there' is.
quotes.source: Shoe Dog: A Memoir by the Creator of Nike (2016)
quotes.notes: Source axis fails: wording is consistent and genuinely from Knight's 1962 'I told myself' passage, but every locatable source is an aggregator; not confirmed against the primary book text.

quotes.content: I only hope that we never lose sight of one thing — that it was all started by a mouse.
quotes.source: 'The Disneyland Story,' premiere of the Disneyland TV series, ABC (Oct. 27, 1954)
quotes.notes: Walt said this on camera on his own TV show, crediting Mickey Mouse as the origin of the enterprise; minor punctuation variants exist across transcriptions.

quotes.content: Failure is only the opportunity more intelligently to begin again.
quotes.source: My Life and Work (1922, with Samuel Crowther)
quotes.notes: The genuine primary wording (confirmed in Project Gutenberg's full text); the popular 'Failure is simply the opportunity to begin again, this time more intelligently' is a paraphrase with no primary source.

quotes.content: I never dreamed about success. I worked for it.
quotes.source: Official Estée Lauder Companies 'Our Founder' heritage page
quotes.notes: Wording is endorsed by the company she founded on its official heritage page; date is approximate as the page does not pin it to a book passage or occasion.

quotes.content: Luck is a dividend of sweat. The more you sweat, the luckier you get.
quotes.source: Attributed to Grinding It Out: The Making of McDonald's (1977)
quotes.notes: Source axis fails: attributed to Kroc's memoir but no primary passage was reachable to confirm it, and wording splits between 'a dividend' and 'the dividend.'
